entries.intro: The old potter near {subject_focus_the} made a set of bowls last week that ring like bells when you tap them. She says she doesn't know how.
details: Same clay, same kiln, same glaze she's used for thirty years. But these bowls vibrate with a clear tone when struck.
Each bowl rings at a different pitch. If you line them up, they form a scale.
She's tried to replicate it four times since. Every new batch comes out normal.
reactions: People are buying them as curiosities. She could charge triple and still sell out.
She's more bothered than pleased. A craftsman who can't explain their own work is a craftsman who can't repeat it.

statCheck.pass: I examined the clay she used that day. It had a streak of something different mixed in: a thin vein of reddish mineral that isn't in her normal supply. It must have washed into the clay pit during the last rain.
statCheck.fail: You tap the bowls and hear the tones, but the clay and glaze look identical to her normal work. Whatever's different is beyond what you can see.

entries.intro: A traveling smith passed through {subject_focus_the} and repaired the village gate hinges in a way nobody here can figure out. They move like they're oiled, but they're dry.
details: He worked for two hours and refused payment. Said the work itself was payment enough.
The joints have no visible pins or rivets. The metal pieces interlock in a way that shouldn't be possible to assemble from the outside.
The hinges don't rust. It's been raining all week and they still gleam.
reactions: Our smith studied them for a full day and said she'd need to take them apart to understand the technique. But she can't figure out how to take them apart.
Generous stranger or not, I'd like to know who he was. Nobody works that well and travels alone without a reputation.

entries.intro: The weaver near {subject_focus_the} found a loom pattern in her late mother's trunk. She tried it, and the cloth it produces repels water completely.
details: Pour a bucket on it and every drop rolls right off. The fibers stay bone dry.
It's the same wool she always uses. The pattern is the difference: a tight, complex weave that takes three times as long to produce.
Her mother never mentioned it. There are notes alongside the pattern, but they're in a shorthand nobody can read.
reactions: She could make a fortune selling waterproof cloth, but the pattern takes so long that she can only produce a few yards a week.
I offered to help her decode the notes. Some of the symbols look like the marks on the old bridge stones, which is odd.

statCheck.pass: I studied the notes more carefully. They're not shorthand: they're an old technical notation for tension and twist. The water resistance comes from the specific angle of each thread crossing. It's engineering, not magic.
statCheck.fail: You look at the mother's notes, but the symbols don't match any writing system you know. Could be personal notation or could be something older.

entries.intro: The carpenter near {subject_focus_the} has been building with a wood nobody can identify. Says he found a fallen tree after the last storm and the timber is unlike anything he's worked before.
details: The grain is so tight you can barely see it. Takes an edge like metal and holds a joint without glue.
It's heavy: heavier than oak, almost as heavy as stone. But it shapes easily under a sharp blade.
The sawdust smells like honey and copper. Strange combination, but not unpleasant.
reactions: He's made a chair from it that I'd swear will outlast the house it sits in.
He went back to where the tree fell to look for more. Found the stump, but no roots. Like the tree had been placed there, not grown.

statCheck.pass: I took a sliver and burned it. The flame was green and the ash was metalite: dense and black. This wood has mineral content woven through its fibers. It grew somewhere with very unusual soil.
statCheck.fail: You examine a piece of the wood closely, but it looks and feels like extremely high-quality hardwood. Without tools, you can't tell what makes it special.

entries.intro: An apprentice glassblower near {subject_focus_the} accidentally created a lens that lets you see things that aren't there. Or things that are there but shouldn't be visible.
details: Look through it at the old foundation stones and you can see markings that aren't visible to the naked eye: carved lines that glow faintly blue.
Look through it at the sky at dusk and you can see thin threads of light connecting the hilltops, like a web.
The apprentice tried to make another one. Same sand, same temperature. The second lens is just normal glass.
reactions: The master glassblower wants to smash it. Says it's a flawed piece and giving people ideas. The apprentice hid it.
I looked through it once. I saw enough to know I don't want to look again. Some things are invisible for a reason.
